communityVoices.quote: The Dark Star performed on this show while not my favourite ever (that would have to go to Live/Dead), is my favourite excluding 1969.
Dark Star is so jazzy (almost Dave Brubeckish) and integrated. I have to say it is one of my favorite versions too.
Veneta was like a family reunion.
They set us on fire for sure on this day
30 Thousand plus downloads says it all...

whyThisShow: This is one of the broadly loved shows from the post-Europe '72 stretch, and reviewers keep returning to the setting as much as the music — a free outdoor concert on what xonix describes as "an absolutely schorching day, children playing everywhere, running up onto the stage in front of the band" [source: 'A free outdoor concert on an absolutely schorching day, children playing everywhere, running up onto the stage in front of the band']. The same reviewer calls it "the show that got me, and many many others, into the Dead" [source: 'This is the show that got me, and many many others, into the Dead']. The Dark Star is the most-cited performance — Tie-Dyed Tom calls it "so jazzy (almost Dave Brubeckish) and integrated" [source: 'Dark Star is so jazzy (almost Dave Brubeckish) and integrated'], and xonix names it their favorite Dark Star excluding 1969 [source: 'is my favourite excluding 1969']. The community treats the recording as a known quantity — one reviewer points to "30 Thousand plus downloads" as the simplest measure of its standing [source: '30 Thousand plus downloads says it all'].
listenFor: The Dark Star is the most-talked-about performance — xonix describes its arc as "starting of really relazed and low key and building to a chaotic yet somehow ordered climax" [source: 'starting of really relazed and low key and building to a chaotic yet somehow ordered climax']. Tie-Dyed Tom clocks the Playing in the Band "topping out at 17 minutes" [source: 'topping out at 17 minutes']. The same reviewer flags Bird Song as "played beautifully" [source: 'Bird Song played beautifully'] and describes a synesthesia moment around He's Gone — "I always feel like I can taste the color blue when I hear it" [source: 'I always feel like I can taste the color blue when I hear it']. xonix specifically credits Jerry's Fender Strat for the tone, noting it "complements the country & western style songs in the setlist so damn well" [source: 'complements the country & western style songs in the setlist so damn well']. Worth knowing going in: the SBD source captures the between-song banter, which Tie-Dyed Tom calls "hysterical" [source: 'The stage banter is hysterical'].
context: This is the late-summer benefit show in Veneta, Oregon, recorded a few months after the band's return from the spring 1972 European tour. The recording in circulation is a soundboard (identifier: gd72-08-27.sbd.orf.3328.sbeok.shnf), which is what most community reviews on Archive evaluate. The show was filmed and later released as the Sunshine Daydream documentary; the audio circulated independently for decades before that. dreatimeland frames its appeal partly through the West Coast scene's connection to the room — "Veneta was like a family reunion" [source: 'Veneta was like a family reunion'].
funFact: xonix's memory of the day stuck on a single image — "everyone relaxing having fun eating icecream, dacin naked" [source: 'everyone relaxing having fun eating icecream, dacin naked'].
headline: Jerry's Fender Strat in the Oregon heat.
